\subsection*{Workshop Chapter 1 (Python)}

Standalone Python notebook for Chapter 1 probability-distribution exercises, mirroring the R workshop flow.

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{import math}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{from scipy.stats import hypergeom, binom, poisson, norm, t, chi2, f}
\end{Verbatim}


\begin{exercise}
\textbf{Hypergeometric distribution}


Equivalent to R \ttblue{dhyper()} / \ttblue{phyper()}: use \ttblue{hypergeom.pmf}, \ttblue{hypergeom.cdf}, and \ttblue{hypergeom.sf}.

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{N = 331}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{M = 17}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{n = 60}

\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{hypergeom.pmf(0, N, M, n), hypergeom.pmf(1, N, M, n)}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{(0.03036497699702399, 0.12145990798810331)}
\end{Verbatim}

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{hypergeom.cdf(1, N, M, n), hypergeom.pmf(0, N, M, n) + hypergeom.pmf(1, N, M, n)}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{(0.1518248849851273, 0.1518248849851273)}
\end{Verbatim}

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{hypergeom.sf(1, N, M, n)}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{0.8481751150149799}
\end{Verbatim}


\end{exercise}

\begin{exercise}
\textbf{Binomial distribution}


Equivalent to R \ttblue{dbinom()} / \ttblue{pbinom()}: use \ttblue{binom.pmf} and \ttblue{binom.cdf}.

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{binom.pmf(k=1, n=60, p=17 / 331), binom.cdf(1, 60, 17 / 331)}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{(0.13733137916836718, 0.17960790177509958)}
\end{Verbatim}


\end{exercise}

\begin{exercise}
\textbf{Poisson distribution}


Equivalent to R \ttblue{dpois()} / \ttblue{ppois()}: use \ttblue{poisson.pmf} and \ttblue{poisson.cdf} with \ttblue{mu = n * pi}.

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{mu = 60 * 17 / 331}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{poisson.pmf(1, mu), poisson.pmf(3, mu)}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{(0.1414043918733124, 0.22379789843860848)}
\end{Verbatim}

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{poisson.cdf(1, mu)}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{0.1872915033537697}
\end{Verbatim}


\end{exercise}

\begin{exercise}
\textbf{Normal distribution}


Equivalent to R \ttblue{pnorm()}: use \ttblue{norm.cdf(x, loc, scale)}.

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{scale = 115.26 / math.sqrt(200)}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{norm.cdf(x=1012, loc=1030, scale=scale)}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{0.013602685762330115}
\end{Verbatim}

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{norm.cdf(1012, 1030, scale), norm.cdf(x=1012, loc=1030, scale=scale), norm.cdf(scale=scale, x=1012, loc=1030)}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{(0.013602685762330115, 0.013602685762330115, 0.013602685762330115)}
\end{Verbatim}


\end{exercise}

\begin{exercise}
\textbf{Student's t distribution}


Equivalent to R \ttblue{pt()}: compute \ttblue{t\_val}, then use \ttblue{t.cdf}.

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{t_val = (1004 - 1030) / (73.8 / math.sqrt(10))}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{t_val, t.cdf(x=t_val, df=9)}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{(-1.114081560492925, 0.14705532622052686)}
\end{Verbatim}


\end{exercise}

\begin{exercise}
\textbf{Chi-squared distribution}


Equivalent to R \ttblue{qchisq()}: use \ttblue{chi2.ppf}.

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{chi2.ppf(q=0.95, df=9), chi2.ppf(q=0.05, df=9)}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{(16.918977604620448, 3.325112843066815)}
\end{Verbatim}


\end{exercise}

\begin{exercise}
\textbf{F distribution}


Equivalent to R \ttblue{qf(..., lower.tail = FALSE)} / \ttblue{pf(..., lower.tail = FALSE)}: use \ttblue{f.ppf} and \ttblue{f.sf}.

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{f_crit = f.ppf(q=0.95, dfn=25, dfd=23)}
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{f_crit}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{1.9962706179379208}
\end{Verbatim}

\begin{Verbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\textcolor{KPMG_blue}{f.sf(x=f_crit, dfn=25, dfd=23)}
\textcolor{KPMG_light_blue}{0.049999999999999975}
\end{Verbatim}

\end{exercise}
